description: One claim 'natheri' another when the first cannot be true, under the stated reading and conditions, while the second is false. The verb names a necessary relation between propositions. Causation, conversational consequence, and persuasive support can all occur without that relation.
articulatory_notes: Set the tongue tip at the upper teeth for voiced dental nasal 'n' and open onto central 'a'. Keep the tongue at the upper teeth for stressed unvoiced 'th', then move into middle-height front 'e'. Finish with a brief alveolar tap or short trill before high-front unrounded 'i'.
usage_notes: The entailing claim is the subject and the entailed claim is the object; the event-noun rule supplies entailment. The direction matters when stating necessary or sufficient conditions. 'ka' names cause, 'thelao' marks a discourse consequence, and 'panoru' names support that need not be conclusive.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Precise entailment makes the conditions and interpretation of necessity visible rather than turning a forceful intuition into an absolute claim.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Participants can distinguish a necessary relation from persuasion or insistence. The distinction exposes pressure disguised as logic.

semantic_domains.cognition: a necessary truth-preserving relation between propositions
